lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — The cold-tumor problem
Now we come to a topic that haunts the field: the cold-tumor problem.
I've been talking, for most of this lecture, as if every tumor has T cells inside it that have been suppressed and are waiting to be unleashed.
That is true for some tumors.
It is decidedly not true for others.
The vocabulary of the field, somewhat unfortunately, divides tumors into hot and cold.
A hot tumor is one that has been heavily infiltrated by T cells — the immune system has noticed it, marched in, and is engaged in a battle that the tumor is currently winning.
Checkpoint inhibitors work in hot tumors because they tip the scales of an ongoing fight.
A cold tumor is one that the immune system has, for whatever reason, never noticed or never reached.
There are no T cells inside.
The tumor is, immunologically, alone.
You can have all the checkpoint inhibitors in the world, you can release every brake in existence, but if there is no T cell present to release, the drug does nothing.
Which cancers are hot?
Melanoma, lung cancer, kidney cancer, bladder cancer, Hodgkin lymphoma, MSI-high tumors, head and neck cancer.
Many of these are tumors driven by mutagenic exposures — UV light, tobacco smoke — that generate enormous mutational burdens and lots of neoantigens.
Or they have other features, like the immune-rich microenvironment of Hodgkin lymphoma, that draw T cells in.
Which cancers are cold?
Pancreatic adenocarcinoma.
Prostate cancer.
Most glioblastoma.
Ovarian cancer.
Microsatellite-stable colorectal cancer — which is most colorectal cancer.
Many breast cancers, particularly hormone-receptor-positive breast cancers.
These tumors have low mutational burdens.
They sit in tissues that may have intrinsic immunosuppression.
They recruit suppressive myeloid cells.
They put up barriers — physical barriers, like the dense fibrotic stroma of pancreatic cancer — that exclude T cells from ever reaching the tumor cells.
The clinical consequence is brutal.
Pancreatic cancer remains essentially incurable in the metastatic setting.
Five-year survival for metastatic pancreatic cancer is single digits.
Multiple large checkpoint-inhibitor trials in pancreatic cancer have failed.
The drugs that have rewritten the survival curves for melanoma and lung cancer have done almost nothing for pancreatic cancer.
Same for most glioblastoma.
Same for hormone-receptor-positive breast cancer.
The biology is the constraint; no amount of cleverness in drug design can rescue a tumor that has no T cells inside it.
So the central question for cold tumors is: how do we make them hot?
How do we get T cells to recognize and infiltrate a tumor that has been ignoring the immune system?
There are several strategies under active investigation.
One: deliver antigen to the tumor directly.
Inject oncolytic viruses, which infect tumor cells and cause them to release their contents in a way the immune system can recognize.
Talimogene laherparepvec — T-VEC — is an approved oncolytic herpes virus for melanoma, and it works in part by making cold lesions hot.
Many similar viruses are in development.
Two: prime T cells outside the tumor with vaccines.
If the immune system has never seen the tumor's antigens, give the patient a vaccine that contains those antigens — mRNA-encoded neoantigens, dendritic-cell vaccines, peptide vaccines.
We'll come back to this in Segment 17.
Three: destroy the physical barriers.
Pancreatic cancer's stroma can be partly broken down by drugs like hyaluronidase, which has been combined with chemotherapy in trials.
Anti-angiogenic drugs can normalize the tumor vasculature and allow T-cell infiltration.
Four: use radiation to spark an immune response.
Radiation kills tumor cells in a way that releases tumor antigens and inflammatory signals — the so-called "in situ vaccination" effect.

Combining local radiation with systemic checkpoint inhibitors is an active area of investigation, with some striking case reports of the so-called "abscopal effect" — where radiation to one tumor lesion shrinks tumors elsewhere in the body, presumably through systemic immune activation.
Five: adoptive cell transfer.
If the patient doesn't have T cells that can find the tumor, manufacture them.
Take T cells out of the patient, engineer them to recognize tumor antigens — CAR-T cells, TCR-T cells, TIL therapy — and reinfuse them.
This is a major topic in its own right, and one of the most active areas of cell therapy development.
None of these strategies has yet cracked the cold-tumor problem.

Pancreatic cancer remains the hardest to crack, and despite hundreds of trials testing checkpoint inhibitors plus this, plus that, plus the other thing, no combination has produced a meaningful survival improvement in the metastatic setting.
But the field is, methodically, working through every plausible angle.
And every few years, a previously cold cancer turns out to have a hot subset — like MSI-high colorectal cancer turning out to be hot, while microsatellite-stable colorectal cancer remains cold.
The boundaries are not static.
Biology keeps surprising us.
The takeaway.
Checkpoint inhibitors are not, by themselves, the answer to cold tumors.
They work on tumors where T cells are already present.
For cold tumors, we need to find ways to get T cells in first, and only then release the brakes.
The next wave of immuno-oncology is in large part about that two-step problem: heat the tumor up, then release the brakes.
